skill.name: unity-ecs-patterns
skill.description: Guides Unity developers through ECS, DOTS, Jobs, Burst, entity queries, baking, command buffers, aspects, and native collection patterns.
skill.summary: Apply production Unity ECS and DOTS patterns for high-performance game systems.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown code fences, Unity API terms, and reference links. Manual review found no executable shell commands, prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent. The remaining network indicator is limited to documentation and sample repository URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are markdown code fences around Unity C# examples. They do not execute commands or invoke a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are visible markdown fences or C# code sample boundaries. No shell interpreter, process launch API, or command string is present at those lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Unity ECS Terms Flagged as Blocker Patterns
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts map to Unity ECS words such as Entity, System, OnCreate, and DestroyEntity in instructional C# examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Unity ECS tutorial code, not cryptographic or reconnaissance behavior. No evidence found of host inspection, weak crypto calls, or malware-like intent.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Present
low_findings.description: The file includes hardcoded links to Unity documentation, Unity ECS samples, and the Burst guide. These are references, not active network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a Resources section. There is no code that fetches those URLs, sends data, or directs the agent to contact external services.

content.value_statement: Unity ECS and DOTS can be hard to apply correctly at scale. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for components, systems, jobs, baking, and Burst-ready performance.
actual_capabilities: Explains Unity ECS concepts, including entities, components, systems, worlds, archetypes, and chunks.
Provides C# patterns for ISystem, IJobEntity, entity queries, and command buffers.
Shows how to use aspects, singleton components, baking, and native collections.
Highlights performance practices for Burst, parallel jobs, structural changes, and chunk utilization.
Helps convert object-oriented Unity gameplay logic into data-oriented ECS designs.
limitations: It is a guidance skill and does not run Unity or compile projects.
Examples may need updates for specific Unity Entities package versions.
It does not cover every DOTS package or multiplayer architecture.
It assumes the user understands basic Unity and C# development.

use_cases.title: Plan an ECS Migration
use_cases.description: Break existing MonoBehaviour gameplay into data components, systems, queries, and command buffer workflows.

use_cases.title: Optimize Large Simulations
use_cases.description: Design Burst-compatible jobs and data layouts for thousands of moving, spawning, or interacting entities.

use_cases.title: Teach DOTS Patterns
use_cases.description: Create practical examples that explain ECS concepts, baking, aspects, native collections, and structural change rules.

prompt_templates.title: Explain ECS Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain Unity ECS using entities, components, systems, archetypes, and chunks. Use a simple enemy movement example.

prompt_templates.title: Convert MonoBehaviour Logic
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer moving existing gameplay to ECS
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this Unity MonoBehaviour gameplay behavior into ECS components, systems, and command buffer usage. Explain each design choice.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Burst Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Burst-compatible ECS job for many entities that need movement, targeting, and health updates. Include data layout recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Review DOTS Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Unity DOTS architecture for performance, structural changes, native collection lifetime, baking design, and query efficiency.

output_examples.input: I need enemies to spawn every second and move forward using Unity ECS.
output: A component plan for spawner data, speed, health, tags, and prefab references.
A system design that uses an entity command buffer for deferred spawning.
Performance notes for avoiding structural changes inside hot loops.

output_examples.input: My ECS movement system is slow with ten thousand entities.
output: A review checklist covering Burst usage, query shape, memory layout, and job scheduling.
Suggestions to move work into IJobEntity or chunk jobs where parallel scheduling helps.
Guidance for profiling before changing architecture.

output_examples.input: How should I bake authoring GameObjects into ECS data?
output: A baking strategy that separates editor authoring data from runtime component data.
Advice on prefab entity references, dependency declarations, and buffer creation.
Warnings about version-specific Entities package behavior.

best_practices: Start with small component sets and clear system ownership before adding advanced jobs.
Use Burst-friendly data and avoid managed references in runtime ECS components.
Profile query costs, chunk utilization, and structural changes before optimizing.
anti_patterns: Do not copy large MonoBehaviour objects directly into ECS components.
Do not perform frequent add, remove, create, or destroy operations in hot paths.
Do not ignore disposal rules for native collections used by jobs or systems.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete Unity projects?
faq.answer: No. It provides ECS patterns, architecture guidance, and example structures that users adapt inside their own Unity projects.

faq.question: Does it support Unity DOTS and Burst?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill focuses on ECS, DOTS, Jobs, Burst, native collections, and data-oriented Unity performance.

faq.question: Can beginners use it?
faq.answer: Yes, but users should know basic Unity and C#. The skill explains ECS concepts before advanced patterns.

faq.question: Can it review existing ECS code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help identify design issues around queries, jobs, command buffers, baking, and native collection lifetime.

faq.question: Does it run commands or access Unity documentation automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill file contains reference links only. It does not include executable scripts or active network requests.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
